claim: FAO reports livestock contribute 30-50 percent of agricultural GDP in East African countries, support 20 million pastoralists, and that diseases including RVF, CBPP, and PPR cause annual losses exceeding 20 percent of herd value in some areas.

source.title: FAO Livestock Sector Briefs: East Africa

method: Reviewed FAO livestock sector briefs and disease-impact assessments for East Africa.
limitations: Disease-impact estimates are modeled and may not capture local variation.
Livestock population data is based on census data of variable recency.

claim: A 2009 study demonstrated that satellite-derived vegetation indices and sea-surface temperature anomalies can predict Rift Valley fever outbreak risk in East Africa with 2-3 month lead time, forming the basis for operational early warning.

source.title: Prediction of a Rift Valley fever outbreak

method: Reviewed RVF prediction model, NDVI-SST methodology, and validation results.
limitations: Model performance varies by region and outbreak magnitude.
Does not predict PPR or CBPP, which have different environmental drivers.

claim: The World Organisation for Animal Health (WOAH) operates WAHIS as the primary global system for official animal disease reporting, collecting outbreak notifications and six-monthly reports from member countries, but reporting completeness, timeliness, and sub-national spatial precision remain variable, particularly in pastoralist regions of East Africa.

source.title: The Science System of the World Organisation for Animal Health

method: Reviewed WOAH WAHIS documentation including the science system overview article and the public WAHIS interface to assess reporting frequency, spatial grain, and known gaps for East African livestock diseases including RVF, PPR, and CBPP.
limitations: WAHIS outbreak reports are country-reported and their completeness depends on national veterinary surveillance capacity, which is uneven across East African countries.
The system provides event-level outbreak coordinates when reported, but many pastoralist areas lack the field surveillance needed to trigger reports in the first place.
Six-monthly reports aggregate at country level and do not provide sub-national disease distribution maps.
The WOAH science system article describes institutional framework and processes, not a systematic assessment of reporting completeness gaps.

claim: Peste des petits ruminants (PPR) is distributed across large parts of Africa, the Middle East, and South Asia, with an estimated 1.7 billion sheep and goats at risk, making PPR a relevant disease family for East African livestock surveillance framing.

source.title: Strategies and Future of Global Eradication of Peste des Petits Ruminants Virus

method: Reviewed the chapter on PPR eradication strategies in the Springer volume Peste des Petits Ruminants Virus, focusing on global distribution, at-risk population estimates, and vaccination strategy.
limitations: The 1.7 billion at-risk figure is a global estimate and does not specify the East African subset.
The chapter was published in 2014 and predates later FAO-WOAH eradication programme updates.
This source does not prove current vaccination coverage or current disease absence in East African pastoralist areas.

claim: Contagious bovine pleuropneumonia (CBPP) remains endemic in sub-Saharan Africa with occurrence reported from at least 25 countries and causes economic losses in affected cattle populations, making CBPP a relevant disease family for East African livestock surveillance framing.
method: Reviewed the CBPP chapter in Transboundary Animal Diseases in Sahelian Africa and Connected Regions, covering disease distribution, economic impact estimates, and surveillance challenges in African endemic regions.
limitations: The chapter covers CBPP across sub-Saharan Africa broadly; East Africa-specific sub-national data is not systematically presented.
Published in 2019; subsequent outbreaks and control campaigns may have changed the endemicity map.
This source does not by itself verify current WAHIS reporting obligations or reporting completeness.

claim: Index-Based Livestock Insurance (IBLI) piloted in Marsabit, Kenya demonstrated that satellite-derived NDVI can predict livestock mortality at index scale, establishing that satellite forage indices are a viable proxy for pastoralist livestock loss and providing the methodological foundation for linking vegetation conditions to livelihood risk.

source.title: Index Based Livestock Insurance for Northern Kenya's Arid and Semi-Arid Lands: The Marsabit Pilot

method: Reviewed the IBLI Marsabit pilot design paper covering index construction from NDVI, predicted livestock mortality validation, and contract payout structure.
limitations: The pilot covers a single Kenyan district and the NDVI-mortality relationship may not transfer to other East African pastoralist systems with different livestock species mixes or vegetation dynamics.
The index measures drought-related mortality, not disease-related mortality; the two causes require separate detection approaches.
SSRN working paper status; the methodology was subsequently validated in peer-reviewed journals but this source is the original pilot design.

claim: The Climate Hazards Group Infrared Precipitation with Stations (CHIRPS) dataset provides quasi-global daily to monthly rainfall estimates at 0.05 degree resolution from 1981 to present, blending satellite cold-cloud-duration data with station observations, and is the standard precipitation input for drought and vegetation monitoring in East African pastoralist regions.

source.title: The quasi-global precipitation time series for drought monitoring

method: Reviewed the USGS Data Series 832 technical documentation for CHIRPS, covering data sources (cold cloud duration, TRMM, station data), spatial and temporal resolution, validation against gauge networks, and known performance in East Africa.
limitations: CHIRPS performance degrades in regions with sparse station networks, which includes much of pastoralist East Africa.
Rainfall estimates are gridded model output, not direct measurement; uncertainty increases in mountainous and arid areas.
The dataset documents precipitation, not vegetation response; NDVI provides the direct forage condition signal.
The 2014 Data Series describes the v1.8 product; subsequent CHIRPS versions may have different performance characteristics.
